title: Lithium Carbonate Intoxication
abstract:
Lithium Carbonate Intoxication
A Case Report and Review of the Literature
Marie Simard, MD; Barry Gumbiner, MD;
Alexander Lee, MD; Howard Lewis, MD; Dean Norman, MD
\s=b\Lithium carbonate is a widely used and invaluable drug
in the treatment and prevention of manic-depressive illness.
However, this medication has a low therapeutic index and,
therefore, many attendant side effects. Acute lithium carbon-
ate intoxication affects predominantly the central nervous
system and the renal system and is potentially lethal. We
studied a case of acute lithium carbonate intoxication char-
acterized by a prolonged comatose state, severe nephrogenic
diabetes insipidus, and glucose intolerance. Of interest, the
patient's symptoms and signs of toxic reaction developed
after normalization of the plasma lithium ion levels. We
reviewed the multisystemic toxic effects of lithium carbonate
as well as its teratogenic potential and guidelines for its use
during pregnancy. The management of acute lithium carbonate
intoxication should include hydration with normal saline,
intensive care monitoring, and dialysis if indicated.
(Arch Intern Med 1989;149:36-46)
Tithium carbonate has been an invaluable drug in the
-*-^ treatment of manic-depressive illness. Treatment re¬
duces or prevents hospitalization of affected patients and
allows them to function in our society. However, lithium
carbonate may induce multisystemic adverse effects and
toxic effects that, when not recognized, may lead to severe,
life-threatening complications. In one series, 15% of pa¬
tients with severe lithium toxic reactions had fatal out¬
comes.1 We describe a patient who recovered from severe
renal and neurologic complications of acute lithium carbon¬
ate intoxication and discuss the management of such
critically ill patients. Included is a detailed review of the
common side effects of lithium carbonate and the life-
threatening complications associated with its intoxication.
REPORT OF A CASE
A 63-year-old man had begun lithium carbonate treatment for
mania in 1969. While he was receiving lithium carbonate, 900 mg

level of 1.0 mmol/L was obtained on Sept 4, 1985. On Sept 13,
1985, the patient was admitted because of alteration in mental
status, nausea, and vomiting. He admitted to polydipsia and
polyuria of several months' duration and denied having increased
his lithium carbonate dosage.
Physical examination revealed an afebrile, lethargic man. Blood
pressure was 110/70 mm Hg; pulse, 84 beats per minute; and
respirations,
16/min. The patient responded to simple verbal
commands, was oriented to place and time, and spoke slowly in
monotones. Neurologic examination results were remarkable for
a slight upper-extremity tremor, bilateral ankle clonus, and un¬
steady gait. The results of physical examination were otherwise
normal.
Laboratory studies disclosed a hemoglobin level of 118 g/L,
hematocrit of 0.38, white blood cell count of 16.0 x 107L, and a
plasma lithium ion level of 4.3 mmol/L. A toxicology screen gave
normal results. Serum sodium level was 139 mmol/L; potassium
level, 4.2 mmol/L; chloride level,
106 mmol/L; glucose level,
5.1 mmol/L; urea nitrogen level, 2.9 mmol/L of urea; creatinine
level, 140 µ    /L; and calcium level, 2.47 mmol/L. Results of
urinalysis were normal, including a specific gravity of 1.015. An
electrocardiogram showed a normal sinus rhythm and nonspecific
ST-segment flattening. A chest roentgenogram was normal.
Vigorous intravenous hydration with normal saline was initiated,
and the patient was admitted to the intensive care unit. Over the
next three days, the plasma lithium ion level declined from 3.7 to
0.9 mmol/L. On the second day of admission, the patient became
stuporous. On the third day, he was comatose, requiring mechan¬
ical ventilation. His neurologic status remained unchanged over
the ensuing nine days. He had minimal response to painful stimuli,
slight nuchal rigidity, doll's eye sign, conjugated lateral deviation
of the eyes to the right and to the left, nystagmoid jerks, and
facial myoclonus. Muscle tone was increased in the lower right
and upper left extremities. Hyperreflexia and bilateral ankle
clonus were present. Computed tomography of the head and a
lumbar puncture were normal. An electroencephalogram recorded
during an episode offacial myoclonus and nystagmoid jerks showed
disorganized, diffuse 2- to 3-c/s delta rhythm with 5- to 7-c/s theta
rhythm and 15- to 20-c/s beta rhythm. Cortical spikes and sharp
wave discharges were absent.
On the second day of admission, the 24-hour urine volume
reached 5.5 L and rose progressively to a maximal volume of 15 L
by the fifth day. Matched amounts of 5% dextrose in water and
0.25% saline solution were administered. The serum sodium level
rose to 145 mmol/L on the second day and peaked on the fourth
day at 172 mmol/L. Hydrochlorothiazide, 100 mg/d, was given
from the fifth to ninth hospital day. Desmopressin acetate therapy

was initiated on the seventh day at a dose of 10 units subcutane-
ously every four hours and was administered for eight consecutive
days. The urine volume declined to 5 L/24 h by the sixth day and
stabilized at 3 to 4 L by the 15th day (Figure). The serum sodium
level normalized by the seventh day. On the fourth hospital day,
the patient developed nonketotic hyperglycemia, with a glucose
level of 23.8 mmol/L, and required 2 to 12 units of regular insulin
subcutaneously daily for the ensuing nine days. For several days,
hypophosphatemia and hypomagnesemia
were corrected with
intravenous potassium phosphate and magnesium sulfate.
On the 13th day the patient's mental status improved. He
followed commands, and facial and eye movements were normal.
He moved all extremities spontaneously, and deep tendon reflexes
were normal. The patient's further hospital course was complicated
by aspiration pneumonia and
an upper gastrointestinal tract
hemorrhage, which responded to medical therapy.
Thyroid function test results and serum cortisol levels were
normal. A water deprivation test performed one month after
admission revealed the following: (1) failure to increase urine
osmolality above 259 mmol/kg of water after 18 hours of water
restriction, (2) inability to conserve water during water depriva¬
tion, (3) inability to concentrate urine over a two-hour test period
after administration of 5 units ofvasopressin given subcutaneously,
and (4)
an elevated basal plasma arginine vasopressin level
(3.6 mU/L) with
an exaggerated
response
to dehydration
(18.2 mU/L). Suppression of plasma arginine vasopressin after
water loading was impaired.
A residual coarse tremor, dysarthria, severe ataxia, and polyuria
resolved completely over a three-month period.
HISTORY
Lithium is a group IA alkali metal with properties similar
to sodium. Investigators have attempted to use lithium
salts for a variety of medical problems, without success. In
the 1940s, it was used excessively as a salt substitute,
resulting in a number of deaths.2 In 1949, an Australian
psychiatrist named Cade noted the calming effect oflithium
carbonate on guinea pigs.2 He observed the same result in
patients with acute manic episodes. Thus, lithium carbon¬
ate began to be used as an agent to treat bipolar mental
illness. However, due to its proven danger when used
improperly, Food and Drug Administration approval was
not granted until 1970, and then with the strictest of
guidelines.3
PHARMACOLOGY
Lithium carbonate is absorbed by the gastrointestinal
tract.4 Peak plasma concentrations are reached within two
to four hours, and steady-state levels are achieved in five
to six days. One third to two thirds of a dose is cleared in
the urine within six to 12 hours, the remainder being
excreted over ten to 14 days. This prolonged excretion
pattern may be related to tissue storage, particularly in
bone.5 Thus, monitoring of serum levels may not reflect
body stores. Because lithium ion is incorporated into red
blood cells via the active sodium-potassium transport
system,6 red blood cell levels may be
a more accurate
reflection of tissue concentrations.
NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS
AND ELECTROENCEPHALOGRAPHY
The neurotoxic effects of lithium salts have been well
documented. Initially, neurologic abnormalities were re¬
ported in patients using a lithium chloride salt substitute.
Seven patients were noted by Corcoran et al7 to have
tremor, blurred vision, muscle fasciculations, hyperre-
flexia, confusion, and coma; lithium salt intoxication was
believed to be a contributory cause of death in two cases.
Since then, the bulk of reported cases have been due to
lithium carbonate ingestion.
Lithium neurotoxic effects tend to develop gradually,
and it may be several days to a week before the full effects
of acute intoxication are manifest.5 The severity of symp¬
toms correlates with serum lithium ion levels.5·8 However,
severe neurotoxic effects despite "therapeutic" lithium ion
levels have been reported.910 The most frequent symptoms
occur with lithium ion levels below 1.5 mmol/L. These
include lethargy, fatigue, weakness, difficulty with memory
and concentration, and fine intention tremor; they may
subside despite continuing lithium carbonate therapy.81112
When lithium ion levels rise above 1.5 mmol/L,
coarse
resting tremor, ataxia, dysarthria, visual disturbances,
brisk deep tendon reflexes, increased muscle tone, muscle
fasciculations, extrapyramidal syndromes, and confusional
states may occur. Above 3.0 mmol/L, the above signs and
symptoms may progress to seizures, coma, flaccid paraly¬
sis, irreversible brain damage, and death.5·811·13·14 Schou
et al5 also reported cases of asymmetric transitory neuro¬
logic deficits, such as conjugate lateral deviation of the
eyes, unilateral extensor plantar reflexes, and lateral ro¬
tation of the head. The acute neurologic effects of lithium
carbonate toxic reactions may last for days to weeks and
may be followed by persistent sequelae.15 Most frequently,
these include cerebellar signs such as dysarthria, tremor,
rigidity, and increased deep tendon reflexes.131516 Long-
lasting choreoathetosis, nystagmus, and ataxia suggest
damage to the basal ganglia.16·17 Deficits in short-term
memory may also be permanent.15·17 Improvement of neu¬
rologic deficits usually occurs within six to 12 months, but
significant improvement after this period is rare.15
In patients with therapeutic serum lithium ion levels,

occur with lithium ion levels below 1.5 mmol/L. These

and concentration, and fine intention tremor; they may
subside despite continuing lithium carbonate therapy.81112

resting tremor, ataxia, dysarthria, visual disturbances,
brisk deep tendon reflexes, increased muscle tone, muscle
fasciculations, extrapyramidal syndromes, and confusional
states may occur. Above 3.0 mmol/L, the above signs and
symptoms may progress to seizures, coma, flaccid paraly¬
sis, irreversible brain damage, and death.5·811·13·14 Schou
et al5 also reported cases of asymmetric transitory neuro¬
logic deficits, such as conjugate lateral deviation of the
eyes, unilateral extensor plantar reflexes, and lateral ro¬
tation of the head. The acute neurologic effects of lithium
carbonate toxic reactions may last for days to weeks and
may be followed by persistent sequelae.15 Most frequently,
these include cerebellar signs such as dysarthria, tremor,
rigidity, and increased deep tendon reflexes.131516 Long-
lasting choreoathetosis, nystagmus, and ataxia suggest
damage to the basal ganglia.16·17 Deficits in short-term
memory may also be permanent.15·17 Improvement of neu¬
rologic deficits usually occurs within six to 12 months, but
significant improvement after this period is rare.15
In patients with therapeutic serum lithium ion levels,
electroencephalographic changes have not been clearly
defined. James and Reilly8 reported a "normalization" of
activity to midrange alpha, with seven patients showing a
decrease and nine an increase in frequency. Slowing of
activity at 3 to 7 c/s has been described by others.18·19
The electroencephalographic changes associated with
lithium carbonate toxic effects have most frequently been
a persistent or intermittent slowing of the dominant fre¬
c/s.8·10 The electroencephalographic
changes may progress in severity as the lithium ion levels
increase, diffuse slowing and an increase in amplitude
being the major changes.20·21 Potentiation of an epileptic
focus has also been observed.7·21
In our patient, the initial lethargy progressed over a
three-day period to coma despite a fall of serum lithium
ion levels into the therapeutic range and correction of the
patient's hypernatremia. Schou et al5 reported that symp¬
toms persist even when serum lithium ion concentrations
have fallen to lower levels because of the low transfer rate
between blood and brain; hence, the lithium ion concentra¬
tion falls more slowly in the brain than in the serum. In
fact, when the concentration of lithium ion in plasma is
lowered by dialysis, recovery is still slow, suggesting that
the intracellular concentration of lithium ion may be the
prime determinant of the appearance of clinical toxic
effects.4 The electroencephalograms obtained in this pa¬
tient showed a disorganized, diffuse 2- to 3-c/s delta rhythm
and 5- to 7-c/s theta rhythm with a 15- to 20-c/s background.
The diffuse, nonsynchronous slowing is consistent with
previous reports of electroencephalographic changes ob¬
served in lithium carbonate-intoxicated patients.9·19·22 The
episodes of facial myoclonus and nystagmoid jerks associ¬
ated with the electroencephalographic findings may signify
a brain-stem myoclonus status. Some authors suggest that
a correlation between electroencephalographic
changes, the serum lithium ion level, and the severity of
neurologic abnormalities.21·22 In this case, the electroen¬
cephalographic changes and neurologic sequelae persisted
despite serum lithium ion levels in the therapeutic range.
A high proportion of patients taking lithium carbonate
develop a urinary concentrating impairment. In studies of
patients treated with long-term lithium carbonate therapy,
70% developed increased thirst23; 40%, polydipsia; 2% to
35%, polyuria (>3 L/24 h)2326; 30% to 90%, lowered maxi¬
mum urine osmolality; and 4.5% to 20%, nephrogenic
diabetes insipidus.23·26 Impairment in urinary concentrating
ability has been shown to correlate with duration oftherapy,
average serum lithium ion level, and total lithium carbonate
dose.26·27 Lithium carbonate-induced nephrogenic diabetes
insipidus has generally been observed to correct within
three weeks after discontinuing the drug.28 However, cases
of persistent nephrogenic diabetes insipidus have been
a case, interstitial nephritis
documented,30 suggesting that irreversibility may be due
to a permanent lithium carbonate-induced nephropathy.
In most patients receiving long-term lithium carbonate
therapy, the decrement in glomerular filtration rate ap¬
pears to be negligible and renal insufficiency infrequent.29
Lithium carbonate may also cause a distal tubular acidosis,
termed incomplete because of the absence of acidemia
A literature survey reported pathologic evidence of mild
renal abnormalities in up to 35% and pronounced structural
renal changes in up to 16% of long-term lithium-carbonate-
treated patients.26 The duration of therapy for patients
with recognized kidney disease in the absence of acute
intoxication was at least two years. Characteristic lesions
include tubular atrophy with dilatation and microcyst
formation; reversible glycogen accumulation in the distal
convoluted tubules and collecting ducts; and sclerosis of
the glomeruli.33 Changes of focal interstitial nephropathy
appear to be nonspecific in that similar changes are seen
on renal biopsy specimens from psychiatric patients not
exposed to lithium carbonate.33·34 Animal studies have
shown lithium carbonate-induced renal collecting tubular
damage to be almost completely reversible after discontin¬
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus appears to be due in
humans25·36 and in rats36-38 to the interference of lithium
carbonate with the mediation of vasopressin stimulation of
adenylate cyclase in the generation of 3'5'cyclic adenosine
monophosphate in the medullary collecting tubule. Also, a
decrease in the corticopapillary gradient of urea has been
implicated in the rat model.37·38 Three patients with lithium
carbonate-induced polyuria responded to exogenous argi¬
nine vasopressin,22·23·39 suggesting that a defect in arginine
vasopressin release may have been present. Lithium ion
has been demonstrated in animals to concentrate in the
pituitary40 and to deplete neurosecretory material from
the pituitary and the suprachiasmatic nuclei.41
Several modes of therapy have been advocated for lith¬
ium-carbonate-induced nephrogenic
Hydrochlorothiazide has been demonstrated paradoxically
to decrease water excretion.42·43 Amiloride hydrochloride
in adjunction to lithium carbonate therapy given to a group
of patients improved renal concentrating ability during a
six-month period of observation.44 Amiloride may blunt the
inhibitory effect of lithium carbonate on water transport
in the renal collecting tubule.44 Indomethacin caused par¬
tial reversal of nephrogenic diabetes insipidus in
patient.45 The lithium carbonate-induced inhibition of ar¬
ginine vasopressin action could be mediated by augmenta¬
tion of prostaglandin activity in the collecting duct cell,
with secondary inhibition of cyclic adenosine monophos¬
phate formation.45 In one patient, carbamazepine success¬
fully treated the resistant lithium carbonate-induced neph¬
rogenic diabetes insipidus concomitantly with the affective
psychosis.46 Carbamazepine may increase renal sensitivity
to arginine vasopressin.47 This compound, however, has
been reported to potentiate lithium carbonate neurotoxic
Before beginning lithium carbonate therapy, patients
should be screened for risk factors for renal disease
(hypertension, hypercalcemia, diabetes mellitus), preex¬
isting renal insufficiency, and concentrating ability. Urine
osmolality after an overnight fast should be measured
before and during lithium carbonate therapy. Patients with
severe renal concentrating defects should have frequent
monitoring of their serum lithium ion levels, as dehydration
can promote lithium carbonate toxic reactions.25
Our patient had been treated with lithium carbonate for
16 years. Worsening of preexisting nephrogenic diabetes
insipidus after dehydration may have occurred,
scribed in a previous case report.43 Also, lithium carbonate
intoxication per se may induce renal tubular damage,26
which in turn may aggravate a preexisting urinary concen¬
trating impairment. The fall in the patient's plasma lithium
ion level from the toxic to the therapeutic range coincided
with deterioration of his renal tubular function. Lithium
ions can accumulate in the distal tubular cells at concen¬
trations approaching ten to 20 times those in plasma.49
There may be an initial delay in the appearance of toxic
symptoms despite high serum lithium ion levels.25 The
patient's basal plasma arginine vasopressin values were
increased and showed an exaggerated response to dehy¬
dration, with an impaired suppression after water load.
vasopressin secretory state characteristic of lithium car¬
bonate-induced polyuria. The lack of effect of exogenous
arginine vasopressin on the patient's high urine output is
also in keeping with nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. Ad¬
marked decline in the 24-hour urine volumes. Hydrochlo¬
rothiazide decreases intracellular volume via sodium de¬
pletion, thereby enhancing résorption of sodium and water
at the proximal tubule. This, in turn, decreases fluid volume
to the distal tubule and collecting duct, which increases
sodium résorption and decreases water excretion.42
The patient's serum urea nitrogen level did not rise
during the first several days in hospital despite his negative
fluid balance. Studies in lithium carbonate-treated rats
have demonstrated a reduced renal corticopapillary gradi¬
ent of urea37·38 and significantly lower plasma urea levels
compared with controls.38 Urine concentrating ability of
the kidneys may be further diminished by
osmolality of renal papillary tissue due to depletion of
urea.37·38 However, the effects of lithium carbonate on the
corticopapillary gradient of urea in the human remain to
MINERAL METABOLISM DISTURBANCES
Lithium carbonate increases renal tubular résorption of
calcium.50 In human and in animal studies, a substantial
hypocalciuria with or without associated hypercalcemia
and without changes in serum or urinary inorganic phos¬
phate levels have been observed.5053 In psychiatrically ill
patients, long-term lithium carbonate therapy has been
shown to increase the mean serum calcium and immuno-
reactive parathyroid hormone levels in comparison with
normal controls5457 or with the patients' values before
initiation oflithium carbonate therapy.68 The hypercalcemia
is usually mild but has been reported to be associated with
calcium levels as high as 3.9 mmol/L59 and may be the only
biochemical abnormality.66·60 Reports in the literature on
the correlation existing between serum lithium ion and
calcium concentrations are conflicting.56·57·61 Lithium car¬
as blunting the calcium feedback
inhibition of parathyroid hormone release;
higher serum calcium concentrations are required to inhibit
parathyroid hormone secretion.62 Brown et al63 elegantly
demonstrated this lithium carbonate effect in vitro in
dispersed bovine parathyroid cells. Under these conditions,
it is speculated that chronic overstimulation of the parathy¬
roid glands may produce independently functioning ade¬
nomas.55 Early renal failure in lithium carbonate-treated
patients may also cause a secondary rise in serum parathy¬
roid hormone and calcium levels.61

LITHIUM CARBONATE AND PREGNANCY
In humans, only case reports and retrospective studies
on lithium carbonate teratogenicity are available. Such
studies have a bias in favor of the abnormality evaluated,
as cases of women receiving lithium carbonate with uncom¬
plicated deliveries and normal infants are underreported.
The concomitant use of other psychotropic drugs may also
interfere with the validity of the data. The International
Registry of Lithium Babies has helped identify possible
congenital abnormalities. Of the data collected, the Scan¬
dinavian contribution is the most complete.184 In 1975, 13
(9.1%) of 143 infants exposed to lithium carbonate in utero
were malformed.186 Eleven had cardiovascular defects, four
of whom notably had Ebstein's anomaly. Similarly, in a
cohort study of pregnant women with manic-depressive
illness, the incidence of perinatal deaths and heart defects
was significantly greater than the predicted rate for this
59 newborns exposed to lithium
carbonate, four (7%) were born with cardiac malformations,
compared with two (0.06%) of 291 without such exposure.
Despite the clinical constraints of such studies, there
appears to be a much higher incidence of cardiac anomalies
among newborn infants exposed to lithium carbonate in
utero than that found in the general population. Animal
studies have shown that teratogenicity in rats, mice, and
rabbits is related to the dose and scheduling of the
medication. When these animals were given therapeutic
doses, their offspring were normal. When given higher
"pulse" doses, which caused lithium ion levels to rise into
the toxic range, cleft palate and ocular abnormalities were
observed.187 Therefore, due to its postulated teratogenic
potential, lithium carbonate should not be used during the
first trimester of pregnancy unless it is deemed clinically
essential.187·188 There have been reports of euthyroid infants
with nontoxic goiters born to mothers with lithium carbon¬
ate-induced nontoxic goiters.89189 The goiters disappeared
over several months after birth. No long-term effects of
lithium carbonate exposure in utero have been found in
children who are normal at birth.190
During pregnancy, maternal glomerular filtration rate
increases markedly,191 thereby increasing lithium ion ex¬
cretion.192 Lithium carbonate dosage should be adjusted on
the basis of plasma levels. A rapid decrease of glomerular
filtration rate occurs at parturition.192 Lithium carbonate
dosage should therefore be reduced either one week before
the projected delivery date193 or at the onset of labor,192 or
discontinued for a few days before and after delivery.188 If
lithium carbonate therapy was initiated during pregnancy,
the dosage should be reduced by one-half at time of
parturition. This decrement would approximate the reduc¬
tion in creatinine clearance.187 Sodium-restricted diets and
diuretics should be used with caution throughout preg¬

Combining other medications with lithium carbonate
may decrease the renal clearance of lithium ions, thereby
causing toxic effects, and, secondly, may result in additive
Diuretics, by causing dehydration or sodium depletion,
are the drugs most commonly associated with lithium
carbonate toxic effects. Thiazide diuretics decrease lithium
ion clearance by increasing sodium loss199 and decreasing
delivery of solute to the distal nephron. This stimulates
increased proximal tubule résorption of not only sodium
but also lithium ions by shared transport mechanisms.200
Potassium-sparing diuretics may also potentiate lithium
carbonate toxic effects, but at a slower rate.200
Not all diuretics interact with lithium carbonate in a
deleterious manner. Furosemide has been shown not to
affect serum lithium ion levels in normal volunteers treated
with both drugs for two weeks.201 Lithium carbonate is
mainly resorbed in the proximal tubule and, to a lesser
degree, in the loop of Henle.200202 Furosemide may block
the résorption of lithium carbonate at this latter site. This
a balance between furosemide's sodium-
wasting effects, causing a rise in lithium ion levels, and its
ability to increase lithium ion clearance. If diuretics are
indicated in patients receiving lithium carbonate, loop
diuretics may be preferable and would obviate the need for
adjustment in the lithium carbonate dose.
Agents that increase glomerular filtration rate will cause
a decrease in serum lithium ion levels. Aminophylline,
urea, sodium bicarbonate, and acetazolamide can increase
clearance by up to 50%.200 Yet, these drugs have not proven
to be efficacious in treating lithium carbonate poisoning.203
Antipsychotic medications, in particular haloperidol and
phenothiazines, may interact in a deleterious manner.204
Patients suffering from an acute manic phase are particu¬
larly at risk for toxic reactions. Two different syndromes
have been described. Lithium carbonate may increase the
dopamine-blocking effects of haloperidol, resulting in a
condition similar to the malignant neuroleptic syndrome.
Phenothiazines potentiate lithium carbonate neurotoxic
effects.205 This is particularly true of thioridazine, a drug
that is very effective in "rapid-cycling" patients uncon¬
trolled by lithium carbonate alone. The proposed mecha¬
nism is an elevation in intracellular lithium ion levels.206 All
these side effects may be dose related. Patients receiving
low and moderate doses of these medications rarely develop
Though less frequently reported, numerous other drugs
may interact adversely with lithium carbonate.22·205 Tricy-
clic antidepressants have on occasion caused toxic drug
interactions. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications,
by decreasing renal clearance through prostaglandin inhi¬
bition, may induce lithium carbonate toxic reactions. Lith¬
ium carbonate combined with electroconvulsant therapy
has been reported to enhance side effects of both treat¬
Toxic effects due to drug interactions may be prevented
by adjusting dosages and close monitoring. Drugs signifi¬
cantly potentiating lithium carbonate toxic reactions, such
as diuretics and antipsychotic agents, should be avoided.
